title: error creating an ID-mapped copy of layer "..."
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When starting two rootful containers that share a base layer in a user namespace, I get an error `Error: error creating container storage: error creating an ID-mapped copy of layer "...": exit status 1: error during chown: error mapping host ID pair idtools.IDPair{UID:..., GID:...} for "..." to container: Host ID ... cannot be mapped to a container ID`.

I can reproduce it in  archlinux VMs using ext4 and btrfs, but not Fedora 33. Happens with `crun` as well as `runc`.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. As root: `podman run --rm -it --uidmap 0:10000:2000 --gidmap 0:10000:2000 --ip 10.88.0.10 --env 'discovery.type=single-node' docker://docker.io/library/elasticsearch:7.8.1`
2. As root: `podman run --rm -it --uidmap 0:12000:2000 --gidmap 0:12000:2000 --ip 10.88.0.11 --env 'SERVER_NAME=10.88.0.11' --env 'ELASTICSEARCH_HOSTS=["http://10.88.0.10:9200/"]' docker://docker.io/kibana:7.8.1`

**Describe the results you received:**

~`Error: error creating container storage: error creating an ID-mapped copy of layer "b97c3d2956a5832284e17580a4a34b8f12d1b8484f62400440b99ff1cb2c9198": exit status 1: error during chown: error mapping host ID pair idtools.IDPair{UID:111000, GID:111000} for "usr/share/terminfo/E/Eterm-color" to container: Host ID 111000 cannot be mapped to a container ID`~

`Error: error creating container storage: error creating an ID-mapped copy of layer "b97c3d2956a5832284e17580a4a34b8f12d1b8484f62400440b99ff1cb2c9198": exit status 1: error during chown: error mapping host ID pair idtools.IDPair{UID:12000, GID:12000} for "usr/share/terminfo/E/Eterm-color" to container: Host ID 12000 cannot be mapped to a container ID`

**Describe the results you expected:**

Two happily running containers.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

The issue does not occur when using the same uidmap/gidmaps. The exact filename differs on each occasion. 

**Output of `podman version`:**

```

```

comments.body:
you are not assigning enough IDs to the user namespace: `Host ID 111000 cannot be mapped to a container ID`.

Can you try mapping that ID as well?

`--uidmap 0:10000:2000 --uidmap 111000:111000:1 --gidmap 0:10000:2000 --gidmap 111000:111000:1`
Hi Giuseppe, I'll investigate further next Monday.

@giuseppe, that's my mistake; I pasted the wrong error message. If I give the first container a uidmap/gidmap of `0:10000:10000`, and the second `0:20000:10000`, I get the error `Host ID 20000 cannot be mapped to a container ID`. I can see there are only files with uid 0 and 1000 within the container when I run the second container on Fedora.

Some more trial and error on my part:

- There's no difference when using cgroupsv1 or cgroupsv2
- It happens in archlinux VMs with btrfs, ext4 and xfs filesystems
- Setting `storage.driver = "overlay"` or `"btrfs"` in /etc/containers/storage.conf makes no difference
- Neither does setting `storage.options.mountopt = "nodev,metacopy=on"` instead of the default `"nodev"`

Interestingly, the error always seems to happen on files that are hardlinked to another; in the kibana container this is true for `/usr/share/terminfo/E/{Eterm,Eterm-color}` as well as `/usr/lib64/distutils/bcppcompiler.{pyc,pyo}`. It makes sense that if [chownByMapsMain][1] changed the owner via one of the two filenames, it would see the changed uid the second time and try to map that, which [toContainer][2] can't.

That doesn't yet explain why this does work on Fedora but not on Arch, is there some kernel option that allows for different owners of hardlinked files?

[1]: https://github.com/containers/storage/blob/9e2666784aaaa95b6ff6a6efc21404f0fd32c558/drivers/chown.go#L60
[2]: https://github.com/containers/storage/blob/53015455b60a41acdd8ab364a087d51c2b08b7b6/pkg/idtools/idtools.go#L104
Just confirmed that on the Fedora VM I can change the owner of one hardlink and leave the other untouched, and on the Arch VM this also changes both the files.

I've since learned that the owner of a file is recorded in the inode, so ordinarily two hardlinks cannot have different owners.

Following @giuseppe's hint in issue #5816 that he was able to reproduce that problem using `index=on`, and based on the hardlink behavior, as well as [the "Non-standard behavior" section in the overlayfs documentation][1], I added this to my `/etc/containers/storage.conf`:

```
[storage.options.overlay]
mountopts = "nodev,index=off"
```

And now I am able to start the two containers in my archlinux VM. Thanks for indulging my rubber duck debugging! :duck:

[1]: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/filesystems/overlayfs.html#non-standard-behavior
